title: Virtual Functions
description: Resolving the cliffhanger from Function Overriding: the virtual keyword, how it enables true runtime polymorphism, and why virtual destructors matter.
objectives: Fix the base-pointer problem left open by Function Overriding
Declare and use a virtual function correctly
Understand, at a high level, how virtual dispatch works
Understand why a base class needs a virtual destructor

sections.text: Function Overriding left this open: overriding alone doesn't make calls through a base class pointer use the derived version. virtual is the fix.

A virtual function is a base class member function marked virtual, telling C++ to decide which version to call at runtime, based on the object's actual type, rather than the pointer or reference's declared type.

sections.title: What changed from the broken version
points: Only one word was added: virtual, on Animal's makeSound()
Now a->makeSound() checks what a actually points to at runtime — a real Dog — and calls Dog's version
This is the mechanism that makes Polymorphism's earlier preview actually work as described

sections.text: Under the hood, a class with virtual functions keeps a hidden table (informally called a vtable) mapping each virtual function to the correct version for that specific type. Calling a virtual function looks up the right version in that table at runtime, instead of the compiler hard-wiring the call to one fixed version while compiling. You don't need to manage this table yourself — the compiler handles it automatically once virtual is used.

This is genuinely important, and a classic interview question: if Animal's destructor (from Destructors) is NOT virtual, then delete a; here would only run Animal's destructor, silently skipping Dog's — a real bug if Dog's destructor was responsible for releasing something like heap memory. Any base class meant to be used through pointers like this should always mark its destructor virtual.

sections.title: A universal remote that checks which TV it's actually facing
sections.text: A non-virtual call is like a universal remote hard-wired to always control one specific brand of TV, no matter which TV it's actually pointed at. A virtual call is a smarter remote that checks, at the moment you press the button, exactly which TV is in front of it, and sends the correct signal for that model — the right behavior for the real device, decided at the last possible moment.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Overriding a function without ever marking the base version virtual — calls through a base pointer will keep using the base version regardless.
Forgetting a virtual destructor on a base class meant to be deleted through a base pointer — the derived class's destructor may be silently skipped.

Assuming virtual is needed on the derived class's override too — only the base class version needs virtual; override on the derived version is a recommended check, not a requirement for the mechanism to work.
Thinking virtual functions are slower in a way that matters for most programs — the runtime lookup has a small cost, but it's rarely the actual bottleneck in typical code.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does the virtual keyword do?
questions.answer: It tells C++ to decide which version of a function to call at runtime based on the object's actual type, rather than the pointer or reference's declared type.

questions.question: Why doesn't calling a function through a base class pointer use the derived version without virtual?
questions.answer: Without virtual, the compiler resolves the call to the base class's version at compile time, based only on the pointer's declared type, ignoring what it actually points to at runtime.

questions.question: Why should a base class have a virtual destructor?
questions.answer: So that deleting a derived object through a base class pointer correctly runs the derived class's destructor as well as the base class's — without it, the derived destructor can be silently skipped, potentially leaking resources.

questions.question: At a high level, how does C++ implement virtual function calls?
questions.answer: Commonly through a hidden table (a vtable) that maps each virtual function to the correct version for the object's actual type, consulted at runtime when the function is called.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: virtual, applied to a base class function, makes C++ resolve calls to it at runtime based on the object's actual type, rather than at compile time based on the pointer or reference's declared type — this is what makes overriding through a base class pointer actually work, fixing the problem left open in Function Overriding. Under the hood, this is commonly implemented with a hidden vtable mapping each virtual function to the correct version per type. A closely related and frequently tested detail is the virtual destructor: a base class intended to be deleted through a base pointer needs a virtual destructor, or the derived class's destructor may be silently skipped, potentially leaking resources it was responsible for releasing.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Virtual Functions
sections.text: virtual = check the real object at the last moment, not the declared pointer type. Base classes used through pointers always need a virtual destructor.
